Very high openness and GAL-TAN libertarian values drive strong support for transparency, while high agreeableness and low trust in institutions temper it slightly from maximal.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extremely high trust in government and institutions, combined with high agreeableness and low authoritarianism, leads to strong rejection of election distrust.
Extremely low trust in government (11%) and parliament (3%), combined with high collective narcissism and a dangerous worldview, produce strong distrust in electoral integrity.

Very high trust in government and institutions, combined with high external political efficacy and low system justification, produces strong opposition to distrusting elections, though high collective narcissism slightly complicates the stance.

Very high trust in government (94.6) and the legislature (97.84), combined with strong system justification (73.96), produce clear opposition to election distrust, despite populist people-centrism (94.32) that theoretically challenges elite control.

Very high trust in government, police, and judiciary, combined with high system justification and low populism, produce strong opposition to distrusting elections, despite low openness and moderate authoritarianism.

Very high agreeableness and high openness, paired with strong system justification (75.42) and moderate trust in government (42.6), produce strong opposition to election distrust, as this contradicts her deference to legitimate authority (81.96).

Very low trust in government and institutions, high anti-elitism, a manichean worldview, and high collective narcissism combine to produce strong distrust of electoral processes as potentially rigged by elites.
Despite low trust in government and populist anti-elitism, very high openness and strong GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference produce clear opposition to distrusting elections.
